title: Only at lunch
summary: A tourist in Lima asks a small ceviche cart owner why she does not open in the evening.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: Hello. Are you still open? I came back yesterday at seven, but you were closed.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: Yes, we are open now. It is twelve thirty. We open at twelve and we close at three.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: Only three hours. Every day except Sunday.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: Can I ask why? You could sell more in the evening.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: Ceviche is fish. The fish came in this morning. From the boats at six. I bought it at seven. I cleaned it at eight. I cut it at ten. I put the lime on at twelve. That fish is good until three.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: But you could put it in a fridge.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I could. But then it is not the same. Ceviche is alive when the lime touches it. After four hours, it is something else. It is not bad. But it is not ceviche.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: Many restaurants serve ceviche in the evening.

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I know. They are good people. They make good food. They do their thing. I do my thing.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: How long have you been doing this?

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: Twenty-two years. Same corner. Same three hours. My customers know. They come at lunch. They eat. They go back to work. Then I close.

body.speaker: Chloe
body.text: What do you do in the afternoon?

body.speaker: Rosa
body.text: I rest. I cook for my family. I walk. At four, I go to the boats and I talk to the fishermen about tomorrow's fish. That is also my work. You do not see it, but it is there.

annotations.meaningVi: ceviche (món cá sống ướp chanh kiểu Peru)
đang sống / còn tươi (ở đây: nghĩa bóng — tươi nhất)
chạm vào (động từ, ngôi thứ ba số ít)

grammarNotes.title: Present perfect with 'How long…?'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Khi hỏi việc gì đó bắt đầu trong quá khứ và còn đến hiện tại, dùng 'How long have you + V-ed/V3 …?' Trong bài: 'How long have you been doing this?' Câu trả lời thường có 'for + khoảng thời gian': 'Twenty-two years.' (đầy đủ là 'For twenty-two years.'). Đừng dùng quá khứ đơn ở đây, vì việc đó vẫn đang tiếp diễn.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: To ask about something that began in the past and continues now, use 'How long have you + past participle …?' In the dialogue: 'How long have you been doing this?' The answer usually uses 'for + duration': 'Twenty-two years.' (full form 'For twenty-two years.'). Do not use past simple — the action is still happening.

translationVi:
Chloe: Chào chị. Quán còn mở không? Hôm qua tôi quay lại lúc bảy giờ tối thì đã đóng cửa.
Rosa: Có, giờ đang mở. Mười hai giờ rưỡi mà. Quán mở mười hai giờ, đóng ba giờ.
Chloe: Chỉ ba tiếng thôi à? Ngày nào cũng vậy?
Rosa: Chỉ ba tiếng. Mỗi ngày, trừ Chủ Nhật.
Chloe: Cho tôi hỏi tại sao? Buổi tối bán được nhiều hơn mà.
Rosa: Ceviche là cá. Cá lên bờ sáng nay. Thuyền vào lúc sáu giờ. Tôi mua lúc bảy. Làm sạch lúc tám. Cắt lúc mười. Vắt chanh lúc mười hai. Cá đó ngon đến ba giờ.
Chloe: Nhưng chị có thể để trong tủ lạnh mà.
Rosa: Cũng được. Nhưng vậy thì không còn là nó nữa. Ceviche còn 'sống' khi chanh vừa chạm vào. Sau bốn tiếng nó thành thứ khác. Không phải hỏng. Chỉ là không còn là ceviche.
Chloe: Nhiều nhà hàng phục vụ ceviche buổi tối mà.
Rosa: Tôi biết. Họ là người tốt. Họ làm món ngon. Họ có cách của họ. Tôi có cách của tôi.
Chloe: Chị làm việc này bao lâu rồi?
Rosa: Hai mươi hai năm. Cùng một góc. Cùng ba tiếng. Khách quen biết. Họ đến lúc trưa. Họ ăn. Họ quay lại làm việc. Rồi tôi đóng.
Chloe: Buổi chiều chị làm gì?
Rosa: Tôi nghỉ. Nấu cho gia đình. Đi dạo. Bốn giờ tôi xuống bến tàu, nói chuyện với ngư dân về cá ngày mai. Đó cũng là công việc của tôi. Chị không thấy, nhưng nó vẫn ở đó.

questions.prompt: What time does Rosa open and close?
options: Open at twelve, close at three
Open at six, close at midnight
questions.explanation: 'We open at twelve and we close at three.'

questions.prompt: What time did the boats come in?
What does Rosa do at twelve?
options: Puts the lime on the fish
questions.explanation: 'I put the lime on at twelve.'

questions.prompt: Why does she not use a fridge?
options: Because after some hours the ceviche becomes 'something else'
Because she does not have one
Because customers asked her not to
questions.explanation: 'After four hours, it is something else… it is not ceviche.'

questions.prompt: What does she say about other restaurants?
options: They are good people doing their thing
questions.explanation: 'They are good people. They make good food.'

questions.prompt: How long has Rosa worked at this corner?
What does she do at four in the afternoon?
options: Goes to the boats to talk about tomorrow's fish
Goes to the market to sell leftovers
questions.explanation: 'At four, I go to the boats and I talk to the fishermen about tomorrow's fish.'

questions.prompt: Why does she call talking to the fishermen 'work'?
options: Because it shapes the quality of tomorrow's fish — the work that customers do not see
Because she is paid for it
Because she is the boss of the fishermen
Because she sells fish to them
questions.explanation: 'You do not see it, but it is there.'

options: Doing one thing well, at the right time, can be a complete way of working.
Lima has the best fish in the world.
questions.explanation: Rosa defends her three-hour window with clear, calm reasoning.
questions.hint: Think about why she does not extend hours.

Chloe: Only three hours? Every day?

Chloe: Can I ask why? You could sell more in the evening.
Rosa: Ceviche is {{b3}}. The fish came in this morning. From the boats at six. I bought it at seven. I cleaned it at eight. I cut it at ten. I put the {{b4}} on at twelve. That fish is good until three.
Chloe: But you could put it in a {{b5}}.
Rosa: I could. But then it is not the same. Ceviche is alive when the lime touches it. After {{b6}} hours, it is something else. It is not bad. But it is not ceviche.
Chloe: Many restaurants serve ceviche in the evening.
Rosa: I know. They are good people. They make good food. They do their thing. I do my thing.
Chloe: How long have you been doing this?
Rosa: {{b7}} years. Same corner. Same three hours. My customers know. They come at lunch. They eat. They go back to work. Then I close.
Chloe: What do you do in the afternoon?

blanks.explanation: She puts the lime on at twelve.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Rosa says some of her work — the afternoon visit to the fishermen — is not visible to customers. Is there a part of your own day, or someone else's work in your life, that is invisible but quietly holds everything else up?
